nativeRange.habitat: Coastal estuaries, river mouths, mangrove swamps, lagoons, and shallow reef-associated areas; typically encountered at depths of about 4–60 m and often within a few kilometres of the sea.

diet.notes: Generally considered a meaty-food carnivore. Has been documented feeding on crustaceans and molluscs in the wild. In captivity, has been known to accept frozen shrimp, krill, clams, mussels, squid, and the occasional live feeder shrimp. Hard-shelled prey such as snails and shell-on shrimp are commonly advised to help wear down the species' continuously growing beak-like teeth.

summary: An Indo-Pacific estuarine puffer with a grey body and dark mottled markings dotted with milky-white spots; generally considered semi-aggressive and best kept singly in a fish-only system with robust tankmates.
careNotes:
Chelonodontops patoca (often still listed under its older synonym Chelonodon patoca in the trade) is a euryhaline puffer that occurs across the Indo-West Pacific, from East Africa east to the Admiralty and Trobriand Islands, north to China and south to northern Australia. It tends to inhabit coastal estuaries, mangroves, lagoons, and river mouths; juveniles are commonly encountered in brackish water and occasionally swim some distance up freshwater streams, while adults are typically found in higher-salinity coastal habitats. Although the species can tolerate a wide salinity range (roughly SG 1.005–1.025 has been reported), most retail sources advise keeping it in full marine conditions (around SG 1.020–1.025), which is what is reflected here.

Reported adult sizes vary somewhat between sources: LiveAquaria lists about 33 cm, Aqua Imports about 25 cm, and FishBase a maximum of 38 cm SL; the range above spans the spread. Minimum tank size is also stated inconsistently — 100 US gallons (~380 L) by LiveAquaria and 125 US gallons (~473 L) by Aqua Imports; 400 L is used here as a cautious round value that accommodates an active swimmer with a relatively large adult size.

Like all members of Tetraodontidae, this species is capable of inflating its body by ingesting water when threatened and should never be allowed to inhale air during handling; a container rather than a net is generally advised for transfers. The flesh and organs contain tetrodotoxin and are highly poisonous to eat, but the species is not generally documented to release skin toxins lethal to tankmates in the way boxfishes or soapfishes can. It is commonly considered semi-aggressive: most sources advise keeping it singly, in a fish-only system with robust tankmates, and avoiding crustaceans, molluscs, corals, and small slow-moving fish. Captive breeding has not been reported in the available literature.
breedingNotes: No documented captive-breeding protocols are available in the consulted sources. The species is oviparous in the wild.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Care sheet: ~13 in adult size, 100 gal minimum, 72–80°F, pH 7.8–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, semi-aggressive, not reef safe, carnivore.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Tetraodontidae/Tetraodontiformes), maximum size (38 cm SL), depth range (4–60 m), Indo-Pacific distribution, brackish/marine/freshwater tolerance, temperature 23–28°C, poisonous-to-eat status, oviparous.

additional.name: Aqua Imports
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (125 gal), adult size (10 in), 75–80°F, pH 7.5–8.5, brackish-to-marine, calm-to-semi-aggressive, carnivore with shell-on prey. Tank size averaged with LiveAquaria's 100 gal and rounded up to 400 L.

additional.name: Wikipedia / hobby summary (Chelonodontops patoca)
additional.notes: Taxonomy fallback: confirmed common-name synonyms (milkspotted toadfish, marbled toad, Gangetic pufferfish/blowfish) and Indo-Pacific range; brackish tolerance ~SG 1.005–1.025 noted.

fish.breedingNotes: Not documented in captivity. Oviparous in the wild.
